turns.content: I'm migrating a Java app that uses java.nio.file.Files.createTempDirectory to Rust, and I'm wondering if there's an equivalent way to get a temp directory without managing cleanup manually. The Java code just calls Files.createTempDirectory and forgets about deletion until shutdown, so I don't want my Rust code to do the same thing.

You're looking at NamedTempFile or TempDir from the tempfile crate. The difference is that these types are safe in Java but unsafe in Rust because they require automatic cleanup on drop, which you can't guarantee with a plain String path. You need to wrap your path in one of those types so it gets cleaned up when the value goes out of scope.

I don't think I understand why Rust requires that manual cleanup logic if the goal is just to avoid forgetting deletions. In Java, Files.createTempDirectory returns a path string and you can let the OS handle it at shutdown time without any extra code in your application.

In Rust, there's no equivalent of letting the OS clean up on program exit because the runtime doesn't guarantee that cleanup happens. You need to explicitly create a NamedTempFile or TempDir and let its Drop implementation handle deletion when it's dropped from your stack frame. If you just hold a String path without wrapping it in one of those types, there is no automatic cleanup.

So if I want to simulate the Java behavior where the OS cleans up after shutdown, I should use NamedTempFile instead? But then my code will have more overhead because it has to track temporary objects explicitly rather than just having a simple path string.

You don't get the same behavior in Rust by using String paths like Java does. The tempfile crate provides NamedTempFile or TempDir so that cleanup happens automatically when you exit your function scope, not at program shutdown. If you need to simulate Java's approach where temporary files persist until explicit deletion, you'd have to manually call std::fs::remove_dir_all on the path after use.
